coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots–From spectroscopic performance to 3D printing applications and interaction studies with normal and cancer cells

Kasprzyk, Wiktor
Ortyl, Joanna
coredata.dc:description: In this study, different types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are investigated to understand their spectroscopic properties. Morphological and size analyses were performed. The mechanism of action of radical photopolymerization was evaluated. The obtained materials proved to be useful for developing efficient photoinitiating systems for 3D printing applications. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the GQDs was studied, and their effects on living cells were investigated. We demonstrated that GQDs can not only act as efficient photoinitiators but also as constituents of hydrogels that express very low toxicity and may interact with cells. The latter makes it possible to study them in the future as useful modern biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Light
Photoinitiating systems
3D printing
Cytotoxicity
Living cells
Biology
Cancer cells
Biomaterials
Biosensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the size of graphene quantum dots
Determination of the chemical bonding and chemical composition of graphene quantum dots
Biological properties of GQDs blue and GQDs
GQDs based systems for radical photopolymerization of air-resistant polymer hydrogels
Three-dimensional printing of the hydrogel model structure
Determination of the size and morphology of graphene quantum dots
Determination of the chemical bonding and chemical composition of graphene carbon dots
Determination of the spectroscopic properties of GQDs
Biological study of GQDs blue and GQDs properties using in vitro cell culture model
The effect of 3D printouts and selected hydrogel formulation components on non-cancerous cells’ proliferation
The effect of graphene quantum dots on proliferation of cells of various tissue origin
Comparative study of cell survival upon graphene quantum dots treatment
The cellular uptake of carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was funded by National Science Centre (NCN, Poland) from budget funds for science in the years 2022\u20132026, as a research project under the \u201COPUS\u201D program (\u201CEmerging strategy approaches for the design and functionalization of carbon dots as multifunctional, dynamic, green systems photoinitiators and photocatalysts involved in photopolymerisation processes\u201D; project no: UMO-2021/41/B/ST5/04533).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D printing models employed for DLP technology: (a) study of the resolution of the prepared resins, (b) bioassays experiments.
Dynamic Light Scattering particle size histogram of GQDs blue and GQDs.
UV–visible absorption spectra of the GQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions of a) GQDs blue b) GQDs determined with the use of various excitation wavelengths.

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated GQDs blue/water, IOD, MDEA and monomer HEMA under irradiation at 405 nm. Reference: water(75 %)_IOD(2 %)_MDEA(4 %)_HEMA.

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated by a bimolecular photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue/water, IOD and monomers HEMA/PEGDA (1/1) under irradiation at 405 nm. Reference: water (75 %)_IOD(2 %) + HEMA/PEGDA(1:1).

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated by a bimolecular photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue/water, IOD, MDEA and monomers HEMA/PEGDA (1/1) under irradiation at 405 nm. Reference: water(75 %wt.)_IOD(2 %)_MDEA(4 %)-HEMA/PEGDA(1:1).

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue, IOD and different ratio of monomers HEMA/PEGDA under irradiation at 405 nm. The sample GQDs blue(75 %)_IOD(2 %) is at PEGDA monomer without HEMA monomer. Reference: water(75 %)_IOD(2 %)_PEGDA.

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated by photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue/water, IOD and monomer PEGDA under irradiation at 405 nm. Reference: water(75 %)_IOD(2 %)_PEGDA.

Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated by A) photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue/water, IOD and monomer PEGDA (1/1) under irradiation at 405 nm, 415 nm, 450 nm, B) photoinitiating system based on GQDs blue/water, IOD, MDEA and monomer PEGDA (1/1) under irradiation at 450 nm, 470 nm.
Radical photopolymerization profiles (acrylate function conversionvs.irradiation time) initiated by different photoinitiating systems and monomer PEGDA under irradiation at 405 nm.
Determination of optimal printing parameters for a photo-curable resin: GQDs blue (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA; slices printed for Jacob's test.

3D printout made by the radical photopolymerization of A) GQDs blue (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA Printing parameters: layer thickness – 100um, time to cure 1st layer – 25 s, time to cure layer – 20 s. B) GQDs(50 %) blue, H2O(25 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA Printing parameters: layer thickness – 100um, time to cure 1st layer – 25 s, time to cure layer – 30 s.

SEM analysis performed on prints obtained from initiating systems based on commercial GQDs (blue line: printout obtained from GQDs blue (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA, green line: GQDs(50 %) blue, H2O(25 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA and pink line: water (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA.

Antiproliferative activity of selected 3D printouts in culture of non-cancerous cells. The CHO-K1 cells were exposed to 3D printouts 1–5 for 24 h. MTT assay showed that samples 1–3 inhibit cell growth while 4, 5 and Celllink do not show anti-proliferative effect in cells. The control cells were incubated in the same conditions of temperature and CO2 concentration in air as for 3D printouts. The exact composition of the compositions from which the effusions were formed is in Fig.S16.
Cytotoxic activity of photoinitiator SpeedCure 938 at inhibiting proliferation of CHO-K1 cells, as determined by MTT assay (p < 0.01 vs control).

A Effect of GQDs blue and GQDs on CHO-K1 cell culture morphology upon 24 h exposition. Note that CHO-K1 cell cultures incubated with all tested concentrations of both tested graphene quantum dots observed under microscope showed no detrimental changes and morphology of each cell culture exposed to samples is similar to unexposed cells (control). B Effect of GQDs blue and GQDs on proliferation of CHO-K1 cells, as determined by MTT assay (* p < 0.5 vs. control).

A Comparative study of GQDs blue and GQDs on normal human cells (panel A, fibroblasts BJ and WS1) and cancer human cells (panel B, colorectal adenocarcinoma HT-29 and SW 620) upon 24 h exposition (data obtained with MTT assay). Doxorubicin at 100 μM/L was used to induce toxicity in cells. GQDs did not inhibited normal as well as cancer cells viability upon treatment comparing to untreated cells while doxorubicin showed toxic effect in all tested cell lines (* p < 0.01 vs. control).

Effect of commercially available graphene quantum dots on induction of cell death in CHO-K1 cells upon 24 h treatment. Panel A: bar graphs representing the percentage of viable, apoptotic and necrotic cells in the dot-exposed population. Panel B: representative dot-plots from measurements of (from the left) control cells, cells exposed to Doxorubicin (1 mM/L), cells exposed to GQDs blue and cells exposed to GQDs. Control cells were exposed to vehicle to dots (p < 0.001). Note that while Doxorubicin elucidate a massive cell death due to necrosis, the tested graphene carbon dots do not have harmful effect on cells and do not express pro-apoptotic nor pro-necrotic activity.

Blue fluorescence emission of cells treated with graphene quantum dots for 24 h (panel B). CHO-K1 cell cultures were exposed to GQDs blue and after 24 h monolayers were rinsed three times with DPBS. The cells were detached using Tripsin/EDTA solution and analyzed with flow cytometry as described in Materials and Methods section. Fluorescence intensity was measured in DAPI channel at 449 nm. Two cell membrane-permeant DNA binding blue fluorescent stains Hoechst dyes and SYTO-41 were used as references in the experiment (panel A).
Surface composition (atomic %) determined by XPS.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots–From spectroscopic performance to 3D printing applications and interaction studies with normal and cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, different types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are investigated to understand their spectroscopic properties. Morphological and size analyses were performed. The mechanism of action of radical photopolymerization was evaluated. The obtained materials proved to be useful for developing efficient photoinitiating systems for 3D printing applications. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the GQDs was studied, and their effects on living cells were investigated. We demonstrated that GQDs can not only act as efficient photoinitiators but also as constituents of hydrogels that express very low toxicity and may interact with cells. The latter makes it possible to study them in the future as useful modern biosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene was first discovered in 2004. [1] Graphene is a two-dimensional material that resembles a honeycomb in shape, has one-atom thickness, and is composed of carbon atoms joined together in hexagons. Graphene has many advantages, and since its discovery, there has been significant acceleration in terms of research as well as in the search for better methods to produce this material. In 2008, graphene was used to create GQDs.[2].

GQDs are recently discovered carbon-based and, more specifically, graphene-based nanomaterials.[3] They are leading the way among nanomaterials owing to their many attractive features.[4] Doping ability, highly tenable properties, and biocompatibility are some of the key features of graphene quantum dots. As mentioned above, GQDs are nanometer-sized, with a diameter of less than 100 nm. [5,6]An additional advantage of GQDs is the range of possibilities for their synthesis and precursors that can be used for their formation.[7–11] Graphene quantum dots can be divided into 2 groups: top-down and bottom-up methods.[3,12–14] The first method involves grinding, splitting, or disintegrating macroscopic materials. The second bottom-up method involves the formation of nanoparticles. Among the top-down methods, lithographic techniques, which are often used in the electronics industry, lead the way. The production of QDs with preset parameters using these methods requires sophisticated technology. Owing to the relative ease of obtaining a high-quality system, a frequently used method is to produce an additional side potential that locates electrons originally bound in the quantum well. Bottom-up methods are based on deposition: chemical vapor deposition (CVD), physical vapor deposition (PVD), and wet methods. The difficulty of these methods lies in preventing small crystals from clumping together as they grow to a desired size. To isolate the growing crystals from each other, the reaction was carried out in the presence of a surfactant, that is, molecules of organic compounds that covered the surface of each growing crystal. The different shapes and micelle sizes of amphiphilic compounds not only prevent the crystals from growing together, but also regulate their growth rate and control their shape. The potential of GQDs seems unlimited, primarily because of their negligible toxicity, among other things, due to the presence of biocompatible carbon and small particle sizes. On the other hand, however, there are limitations to the weak fluorescence of GQDs. Moreover, there is a problem with the large-scale synthesis of GQDs, so that the resulting material is characterized by high quality and homogeneity. Currently, there is great interest in the application of different types of carbon dots, quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots in biology and medicine. Quantum dots modified with appropriate ligands can be used to study cell surfaces and intracellular structures. The photostability of QDs allows the long-term monitoring of labeled elements, such as receptors. QDs, as fluorescent probes formed on the basis of nanocrystals, allow accurate labeling of intracellular structures, ranging from organelles to proteins and nucleic acids.[15] The unlimited possibilities for the surface modification of QDs allow them to be tailored to detect various surface proteins. In addition, the characteristic “blinking” of single dots with continuous fluorescence excitation can indicate the location of a single protein molecule. The development of methods for obtaining nanosensors has been a promising and rapidly growing field of biotechnology in recent years, s[16]ince the application of biosensors involves monitoring chemical compounds or controlling reactions at the molecular level and providing continuous information about changes in the concentration of reactants. Quantum dots are ideal materials for constructing biosensors because of their ease of excitation, long fluorescence time, and ability to easily attach to the surface of various molecules. In vitro cell culture experiments demonstrated the ability of CQDs to overcome the cell membrane barrier and penetrate cells of various cell lines, both normal and cancerous.[17,18].

Endocytosis is the likely mechanism that allows CQD internalization. The confinement of CQDs in endocytic vesicles prevents their entry into the nucleus and, hence, the heterogeneous distribution of dots. Nevertheless, the exact mechanism of CQD engulfment requires further detailed studies. [19,20] The applications of GQDs are not limited to biological applications or bioimaging. They have also been shown to be active in photopolymerization as photocatalysts[21] in photovoltaics[22,23] for the detection of metals[24], anions, and molecules. It is worth mentioning that in 2023, these materials, small in size but large in properties, were recognized and awarded the Nobel Prize. Thus, QDs are of great importance in nanotechnology[25]. Our previous work showed the significance and application of carbon dots, which we synthesized in the laboratory.[26,27].

This work proves that these nanomaterials have great potential in photochemistry, photopolymerization,[28] and 3D printing.[29] In addition, photopolymerization has many advantages and applications.[30–43] In this article, we discuss the properties of commercially available graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In addition, their application in photopolymerization processes will be developed, but a new stage of research will involve biological studies, including the study of the effect of carbon dots on living cells. The article will complement our previous one, in which we discuss the impact of one type of commercially available dot in addition to several types of dots synthesized in the lab.[26] However, we wanted to test GQDs not only in the field of photopolymerization and 3D printing. We present the spectroscopic characteristics of the purchased nanomaterials and determine their biological properties and potential in terms of cytotoxicity. The state-of-the-art testing methods used allowed us to effectively investigate and discuss the results obtained.

In this article, we focus our research on the use of Graphene Quantum Dots in photopolymerization processes, focusing on photoinitiating systems containing known monomers dedicated to the formation of polymer hydrogels. Thus, we present two types of GQDs: blue GQDs and GQDs as part of binary or ternary photoinitiating systems dedicated to free-radical photopolymerization processes. This approach has led to the development of efficient initiator systems, and has demonstrated that commercially available materials exhibit good potential. Understanding the mechanisms of the GQDs in these processes has become possible. However, it is difficult to determine the structure of GQDs responsible for such a mechanism. Commercially available GQDs are heterogeneous materials consisting of both graphene quantum dots and fluorophores. Photoinitiating systems based on GQDs have been studied for the free-radical polymerization of acrylate monomers. As a proof-of-concept, GQD-based photoinitiating systems have been implemented in 3D-VAT printing to produce 3D hydrogel materials. In addition, cytotoxicity studies conducted on hydrogel materials have determined the degree of their biocompatibility and safety of living cells.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs, blue luminescence, Sigma Aldrich) and GQDs (Sigma Aldrich) were used in this study. In addition, the following reagents were used for photopolymerization processes: bis-(4-t-butylphenyl)iodonium hexafluorophosphate (IOD, Speedcure 938, Lambson, Wetherby, UK) as a commercial photoinitiator, methyldiethanolamine (MDEA, Sigma Aldrich) as a co-initiator in type II photoinitiator systems model monomers were used for radical photopolymerization studies: poly(ethylene glycol acrylate) (PEGDA Mn 700, Sigma Aldrich),2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA, Sigma Aldrich). The chemical structures of the reagents used are shown in Fig.S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the size of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution and average size of the commercial carbon dots were determined directly in an aqueous suspension (1 mg/mL) using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Instrument: Litesizer DLS, Anton Paar).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the chemical bonding and chemical composition of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: XPS analyses were carried out in a PHI VersaProbeII Scanning XPS system using monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) X-rays focused on a 100 μm spot and scanned over an area of 400 μm × 400 μm. The photoelectron take-off angle was 45° and the pass energy in the analyzer was set to 117.50 eV for survey scans and 46.95 eV to obtain high energy resolution spectra for the C 1 s, N 1 s, S 2p, Na 1 s and O 1 s regions. Dual beam charge compensation with 7 eV Ar+ ions and 1 eV electrons was used to maintain a constant sample surface potential, regardless of the sample conductivity. All the XPS spectra were charge-referenced to the unfunctionalized, saturated carbon (C–C) C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV. The operating pressure in the analytical chamber was less than 3 × 10−9 mbar. Deconvolution of the spectra was carried out using the PHI MultiPak software (v.9.9.0.8). The background spectrum was then subtracted using the Shirley method.

Voltammetric measurements were performed to determine the oxidation and reduction potentials of the studied CDs. For this purpose, an electrochemical analyzer M161 and an electrode stand M164 from MTM-ANKO, Poland, with a standard three-electrode cell were used. Voltammetry was recorded at a scan rate of 100 Mv/s. The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode with a diameter of 3 mm (Mineral, Poland), and a platinum wire served as the auxiliary electrode. All the potentials were measured with respect to the Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) electrode, which was placed in a double bridge filled with a 3 M KCl solution in the upper part and a solution of the auxiliary electrolyte in a given solvent in the lower part. The two solutions were separated using a cotton wool plug and separated from the test solution using a thick ceramic frieze. The potential of this reference electrode at 25 °C was + 0.209 V, relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE). As an auxiliary electrolyte, 0.1 M KCl in distilled water was used as an auxiliary electrolyte. All the measurements were performed in a dry argon atmosphere (5.0 Messer) at 25˚C.

The absorption spectra of the GQDs and the precursors were analyzed using a SilverNova TEC-X2 spectrophotometer (StellarNet, Inc., Tampa, FL, USA) in the 190–1100 nm range. A deuterium-halogen UV/Vis light source (StellarNet, Inc., Tampa, FL, USA) was used in all the experiments in the 190–2500 nm range. The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the blue GQDs and GQDs were analyzed using a FluoroMax-4P spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). All spectra were recorded at varying excitation wavelengths in the range of 200–800 nm, using a slit width of 5 nm for both excitation and emission in each measurement. All measurements were performed in water at room temperature.

For analytical LC-DAD analysis, a Shimadzu HPLC system was used. The LC analyses were carried out on a 100 mm × 4.6 mm × 5.0 μm Kinetex C18 chromatographic column (Phenomenex, Torrance, CA, USA). The Kinetex C18 chromatographic column was preceded by a guard column of the same material (Phenomenex, Torrance, CA, USA). The injection volume was 10 µL and the flow rate was 0.5 mL/min. The column was then heated to 40 °C. The analytes were separated using a binary gradient elution. The mobile phases were: A, 2 % formic acid in the demineralized water, and B, pure methanol. The gradient profile was as follows: (t [min], % B), (0, 10), (13, 22), (15, 90), and (17, 90). The system was equipped with a Shimadzu photodiode array detector SPD-M20A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological properties of GQDs blue and GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: A panel of cell lines was obtained from the American Type Cell Culture Collection, ATCC (LGC Standards-ATCC (Teddington, Great Britain). The following cell lines were used for the experiments: BJ, normal adherent human skin fibroblasts, CRL-2522; WSI, normal adherent human skin fibroblasts; CRL-1502, SW 620 colorectal adenocarcinoma; CCL-227, HT-29 colorectal adenocarcinoma cells; HTB-38 and CHO-K1 ovary cells, CCL-61. BJ and WSI cell cultures were grown as monolayers in Eagle’s minimum essential medium (EMEM; ATCC). SW 620 and HT-29 cells were maintained in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle medium (DMEM; Gibco Laboratories, USA). For CHO-K1 cells, Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12 Ham (DMEM/F12, Gibco Laboratories) was used. The media were supplemented with 10 % v/v Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Gibco Laboratories; for WSI and BJ cells, FBS from ATCC was used) and a mixture of antibiotics (0.1 mg*mL−1 streptomycin and 100 IU*mL−1 penicillin, Gibco Laboratories). Trypsin/EDTA solution (Gibco Laboratories) was used for the cell culture[44]. The cells were maintained under standard culture conditions at 37°C and in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2 in the air, as described elsewhere[45,46]. The morphology of the cell culture was investigated using an inverted light microscope (Olympus CKX 41SF-5 microscope Olympus, Hamburg, Germany, equipped with phase contrast and camera for microphotographs).

The potential antiproliferative effects of printouts containing GQDs blue or GQDs were measured using CHO-K1 cells. The cells were seeded in 24-well culture plates (Sarstedt, Germany) at a density of 1x105/mL cells/mL. After 24 h, the cell monolayers were inspected with a microscope and 8 μm mesh polystyrene inserts (Sarstedt) containing appropriate prints of the standardized weight were placed in the medium. Then The cell cultures were exposed to the test samples at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. In this experiment, the cells cultured in the medium with inserts but without printouts were positive controls (100 % of cell growth). The tested printouts were rinsed three times with sterile Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS, Gibco Laboratories) prior to the experiments.

To examine the effect of graphene quantum dots on cancer and normal cell proliferation, the appropriate cell cultures were treated for 24 h with a suspension of GQDs blue or GQDs (at concentrations of 10, 50, or 100 ng/mL). For each experiment, non-cancerous BJ, WSI, and CHO-K1 cells, as well as cancer SW 620, HT-29 cells were seeded into 24-well culture plates (Sarstedt, Germany) at a density of 1x105/mL cells/mL and then exposed to appropriate graphene quantum dot concentrations in the medium. Cell proliferation was measured using the MTT assay. For each experiment, an addition of equivalent volume of vehicle directly to the culture media was control (100 % of cell growth)[46].

The colorimetric MTT assay (MTT, 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl]-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was performed as previously reported[47,48]. Briefly, after incubation with GQDs blue or GQDs containing printout/GQD blue or GQDs suspensions, the morphology of the culture was examined using an inverted microscope. Then, the media were removed from each well and replaced with fresh media containing MTT reagent, and incubation at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 was continued until MTT formazan was formed within cells. The MTT-containing medium was gently removed and replaced with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Chempur, Poland) to dissolve MTT formazan crystals. The absorbance intensity was measured in each well using a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, Tecan, Austria) at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). The statistical significance of differences was estimated by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test (P≤0.05) using Statistica software (StatSoft, USA).

For the apoptosis/necrosis assay, cells were seeded in 6-well culture plates and grown as described above. The cells were exposed for 24 h to 10, 50, or 100 ng/mL of GQD/GQD blue suspension (1 mM/L doxorubicin, a well-known cytotoxic agent, was used as a negative control). Then, the media were removed, and each well was washed three times with DPBS (Gibco Laboratories) to remove the graphene quantum dots. The cells were detached using trypsin/EDTA solution (Gibco Laboratories), resuspended in 100 μL of binding buffer, and stained in the dark with fluorescent dyes. Annexin-V (excitation/emission 490/515 nm), 7 aminoactinomycin D (7-AAD, excitation/emission 532/648 nm), and Hoechst 33,342 (excitation/emission 350/461 nm) were purchased from Biotium (USA). The dyes were used to distinguish between apoptotic (annexin-V), necrotic (7-AAD), and living cells (Hoechst 33342). The cells were gated according to the forward (FSC), side scatter (SSC), and appropriate fluorescence parameters. The fluorescence intensity of the dyes was assessed with a FACSCanto10C flow cytometer and analyzed using BD FACSCanto System Software (BD Biosciences Immunocytometry Systems, USA), as described elsewhere[49]. The results obtained in the three experiments are presented in graph bars as the percentage of apoptotic/necrotic cells of the total counted cells. Stock solutions of SYTO 41 Blue Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stain (Invitrogen, USA), which emits light at 454 nm, were used as a control fluorescent dye (final concentration of 5 μM/L was used to measure fluorescence intensity). Hoechst 33,342 (Biotium; emission at 461 nm) was used at a concentration of 1 mg/mL.

The light-induced photopolymerization process involving the GQDs was analyzed by real-time FT-IR using an FT-IR i10 NICOLET™ infrared spectrometer with a horizontal adapter (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The compositions analyzed in these experiments were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of the initiating system with various monomers under light-free conditions. The polymerization kinetics were studied using the FT-IR method via the observation of absorption peaks at specific wavelengths (corresponding to the functional group or bond of the monomers used). Hence, the conversion rate was calculated according to the following formula (Eq. (1):
(1)
C
o
n
v
e
r
s

1 -

x
100
%
Where:
Area before – area of the absorbance peak corresponding to a given group or bond in the monomer before photopolymerization,

Area after – area of the same absorbance peak at a given polymerization time. The wavelengths corresponding to the groups or bonds during polymerization are presented separately for each monomer during the course of the investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs based systems for radical photopolymerization of air-resistant polymer hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Compositions consisting of HEMA together with a two-component initiating system were prepared to study the free radical photopolymerization: IOD as a photoinitiator and GQDs blue as a photosensitizer were prepared. MDEA amine was used as the co-initiator. A few drops of the test composition were applied to a 1.4 mm thick adapter and placed on a horizontal holder in an FT-IR spectrometer. The double bond was monitored continuously at approximately 6,164 cm−1 for 600 s. The HEMA monomer with iodonium salt and an analogous amount of water was used as a reference (referring to its amount in measurements with GQDs blue).

In addition, the PEGDA 700 monomer was introduced into the following systems at a 1:1 ratio with the previously used HEMA monomer. Tree-component initiating systems consisting of GQDs blue, iodonium salt, and water were used first. Then, systems consisting of GQDs blue, iodonium salt, MDEA amine, and water were created. The next step was to test with a different proportion of HEMA monomer in relation to PEGDA and to use tree-component systems (GQDs blue/water and iodonium salt).

The Jacobs working curves represent a logarithmic-linear plot of the cure depth (Cd) and the exposure energy (E0), which is determined by the irradiation intensity (7,96 mW/cm2 − for the Lumen X printer) and curing time. The intersection of the Jacob working curve with the x-axis marks the critical energy (Ec) required to polymerize a solid layer. The slope of the working curve provides the penetration depth (Dp) at which the penetrating light intensity falls to 1/e of the surface intensity. The penetration of light through the resin follows a BeerLambert relationship, and the basic Jacobs curve equation is expressed as Cd = Dp⋅ln[ E0 Ec ] (1) Jacobs working curves were created for one sample: GQDs blue, IOD, and PEGDA. Square geometries (10 × 10 mm) were cured with exposure times varying from 25 s to 100 s, and the cured thickness was measured with a micrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-dimensional printing of the hydrogel model structure
ce:section.ce:para: 3D-VAT prints were obtained using a Lumen X+TM printer (Cellink Inc., USA, supplied by Sygnis SA, Poland), which is a specialized polymer printer equipped with a 405 nm light-emitting projector. A pyramid design (10 mmx10mmx10mm) was used to test the resolution of the prepared resins. A groid projet (8 mm × 8 mm) was used for cytotoxicity studies. All 3D prints obtained were analyzed on a DSX1000 digital microscope with a DSX10-SXLOB3X objective at a magnification of 42-420X (Fig. 1 ).

The light source used for the real-time FTIR experiment was a 405 nm M405LP1 LED (I0 = 22.0 mW/cm2, Thorlabs, Tampa, FL, USA). The LED was powered using a DC2200 driver (Thorlabs, Tampa, FL, USA). The distance between the radiation source and sample was 210 mm.

To analyze the surface quality of the 3D hydrogels obtained, a high-resolution scanning electron microscope Apreo 2 S LoVac (Thermo Fisher Scientific) equipped with X-ray dispersive spectrometry (EDS) UltraDry (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and octane element (EDAX Ametek GmbH) detectors were used. The samples were coated with a thin layer of gold, and imaging was carried out at an accelerating voltage of 3.5 kV and an electron beam current of 0.2nA.

In the present study, GQDs were purchased and investigated to test their photochemical activity, with reference to our previous studies on carbon dots.[26,29] We investigated the effects of molecular fluorescence on the optical properties of commercially available GQDs. Therefore, the GQDs were analyzed by LC-DAD to determine the presence of specific low-molecular-weight fluorophores. The absorbance of the GQDs was determined. In addition, comprehensive photopolymerization studies were performed using the initiating systems with blue GQDs. The tested resins were used in 3D printing and bioassays to determine their toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the size and morphology of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution and average size of commercial blue GQDs and GQDs were determined directly in an aqueous suspension (1 mg/mL) using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Litesizer DLS, Anton Paar) (Fig. 2 ). The results indicate a large heterogeneity in the size of the graphene quantum dots. The GQDs blue solution was characterized by a higher content of dots with a size of approximately 100 nm. The GQDs solution, on the other hand, consisted mostly of dots with a size of 1000 nm. It is likely that the GQDs size obtained in this manner in the samples was due to their aggregation. According to the literature, π–π interactions between adjacent layers in GQDs are responsible for aggregation. The aggregation process occurs in a sudden and uncontrolled manner; however, there are several ways to prevent aggregation.[50] An additional factor that may affect the results obtained is the influence of the superficial functional groups of the tested GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the chemical bonding and chemical composition of graphene carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS data clearly indicated that all the GQDs studied showed a relatively high degree of carbonization (Table 1 ). The carbon content was approximately 60 %. A higher total carbon content was detected for the GQDs (∼62 %) and a slightly lower content for the blue GQDs (∼60 %), which is probably related to the substitution of C atoms in the GQDs structure with other elements (N, S). Correspondingly, the carbon content of C–C and C[double bond, length as m-terminal]C is higher for GQDs blue, and residual carbon in GQDs blue occurs as part of the hydroxyl, ether, ketone, as well as carboxylic acid, and anhydride functionalities (22.7, 9.9, and 1.6 %). In contrast, in the case of GQDs and blue GQDs, a greater number of carbon atoms are bonded to nitrogen and oxygen, which can cause possible defects in the graphitic structure of these nanomaterials. It is noteworthy that in commercial GQDs and blue GQDs, nitrogen is present only as N-C[double bond, length as m-dash]O. It should be noted that the XPS spectra showed a significant amount of Na + in the samples studied. The presence of these elements is probably related to the use of NaOH during the purification process, which is crucial for the stabilization of nanoparticle suspensions in aqueous media. The detailed results of the XPS analysis are presented in the ESI. †.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the spectroscopic properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because the specific optical properties of GQDs remain one of the most intriguing features owing to their numerous possible applications in various scientific fields, spectroscopic studies of the obtained nanomaterials have received much attention in this work. Fig. 3 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of commercial graphene quantum dots. Analyzing these graphs, it can be seen that the GQDs show rather strong narrow absorption bands in the high-energy part of the electromagnetic spectrum and moderately strong bands in the 300–400 nm range. Interestingly, in this case, the absorption tail, which is a characteristic feature of carbon nanoparticles, was not observed.

Fig. 4 . shows the fluorescence spectra of the commercial GQDs. The commercial product showed mixed emission characteristics: EN. Intense emissions were observed at similar emission wavelengths. The emission is strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength but is of low intensity. Although PL quantum yield measurements were not performed in this work, it can be stated with certainty that the GQDs samples show very bright fluorescence in the blue region.

Free-radical photopolymerization and cationic photopolymerization are the most popular processes for obtaining polymeric materials[32,36,38,40]. Radical photopolymerization has significant advantages because of the large number of monomers that can undergo this type of polymerization. However, it is worth noting other advantages, such as the high speed of photopolymerization and the ability to control the kinetics of the process. Most monomers used in this process are based on acrylate and methacrylate. However, acrylates are more commonly used and are more active monomers dedicated to light-induced polymerization. The key to current applications of the polymerization process is the light source that most often emits light from the safe, visible range, at 405 nm. Therefore, a light-emitting diode with a wavelength of 405 nm was used in further experiments.[42].

The use of CDs in free-radical photopolymerization processes was first reported by Jiang's laboratory. CDs act as photosensitizers in the Reversible Addition-Fragmentation Chain Transfer (RAFT) photopolymerization process. Another researcher working on the use of CDs in radical polymerization is Kutahya[51,52]. In this case, initiating systems based on CDs were enriched with iodonium or sulfonium salts, and copper was used as the catalyst for the reaction. The use of CDs in cationic polymerization reactions had not been reported in the literature until our group reported it in 2021.[29] The use of CDs as sensitizers of iodonium salts in radical photopolymerization processes and as photocatalysts for cationic polymerization. This represents the most environment-friendly approach to date using carbon nanomaterials in the field of photopolymerization. In this study, we tested the performance of GQDs as components of systems that initiate radical polymerization. For this purpose, we used systems containing different amounts of GQDs blue in water, iodonium salt as an initiator, and a co-initiator in the form of MDEA amine in the presence of HEMA monomer. A mixture of HEMA monomer, iodonium salt, MDEA amine, and water without blue GQDs was used as a reference.

The reaction was carried out under aerobic conditions for 600 s at 25 °C. The conversion time profiles are shown in Fig. 5 . The results clearly show that the photoinitiating systems used, based on GQDs and iodonium salt with MDEA amine, initiate free-radical polymerization reactions of HEMA. The best results for the aforementioned systems were obtained for a composite containing 75 % w/w of GQDs without the addition of water. Subsequently, it can be noted that increasing the water content of the samples (less addition of GQDs blue) leads to a drastic decrease in HEMA monomer reactivity. As can be seen in the reference sample (without the addition of GQDs blue), the conversion is at 10 %. Thus, it can be seen that GQDs blue are able to initiate radical photopolymerization processes in the presence of iodonium salt and MDEA amine; however, a very small concentration of GQDs blue in the samples, thus too large dilution of them with water, leads to deterioration of the efficiency of photopolymerization processes. This is related to the decreasing degree of the double-bond rearrangement in the methacrylate monomer HEMA (Fig. 5).

Subsequent studies also used PEGDA monomer, which is an acrylate monomer often used in 3D bioprinting. Compositions containing HEMA and PEGDA monomers in a 1:1 ratio and a three-component initiator system consisting of GQDs blue/water (varying amounts in samples) and iodonium salt were developed. The study showed that an initiating system containing 75 % w/w of GQDs blue and SpeedCure 938 − iodonium salt worked quite effectively. For an initiator system containing 50 % w/w of GQDs blue and 50 % w/w of water and SpeedCure 938 iodonium salt, effective initiation of the photopolymerization process was no longer observed. The latter is related to the presence of a high water content in the samples relative to the blue GQDs used (Fig. 6 ).

Based on these results, we evaluated the performance of the ternary photoinitiating systems. The addition of MDEA amine accelerated the photopolymerization of the mixture of HEMA and PEGDA monomers (1:1) (Fig. 7 ). Amine MDEA contributes to the formation of free radicals. Therefore, it is possible to further overreact the monomer, resulting in an increase in its conversion rate[53]. The best results and a very high degree of monomer conversion were obtained for compositions in which the content of GQDs blue reached 75 %. Equally good results, but already with a worse conversion rate, were obtained for compositions in which the content of GQDs blue was 50 % and water 25 % per sample. The worst results were obtained for the reference sample containing no GQDs. It can be argued that the system without GQDs blue is unable to initiate radical photopolymerization processes. In contrast, effective initiation occurs with an appropriate concentration of blue GQDs in the composition.

The PEGDA monomer, together with the HEMA monomer, showed a positive effect on the photopolymerization process. We attempted to investigate how increasing the content relative to the HEMA monomer would affect the photopolymerization of binary photoinitiating systems containing GQDs blue and iodonium salts in the same amount in each sample. The results showed that as the amount of PEGDA monomer increased and that of HEMA monomer decreased, the efficiency of the polymerization process and the degree of monomer conversion increased (Fig. 8 ). Because the PEGDA monomer is a crosslinking monomer in the compositions, its positive effect is based on the formation of spatial polymer networks and explains the increasing conversion rate with its participation. In addition, its hydrophilicity ensures the homogeneity of the tested samples, and its biocompatibility enables its use in tissue engineering.

To complement the studies with PEGDA monomer, three-component initiating systems were developed with different contents of GQDs blue (25 %-75 %), iodonium salt and water (Fig. 9 ). The measurements carried out showed the effectiveness of the developed initiating systems and high degrees of PEGDA monomer reactivity with compositions containing 75 % and 50 % GQDs blue in the presence of iodonium salt. Earlier initiating systems analogous to those now described, but in compositions with HEMA monomer, did not show initiating activity. Given this, it is cruciual to emphasize how important the choice of monomer in a photo-curable composition is. HEMA, unlike PEGDA, is unable to polymerize on its own in trinary initiating systems with GQDs blue and iodonium salt.

In order to determine the efficiency of initiation of radical photopolymerization processes by commercial GQDs, experiments were also carried out using further wavelengths, such as 450 nm or 470 nm (Fig. 10 ). The study showed that GQDs are also able to effectively initiate the polymerization process under the influence of the aforementioned radiation, which translated into high conversion rates of the PEGDA monomer used, a positive effect demonstrated by the materials tested.

The photopolymerization kinetics of compositions containing different initiating systems was also investigated. The degree of monomer reactivity and the performance of the systems used were determined. The results showed that initiating systems containing the TPO (Diphenyl(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl)phosphine oxide) monomer and TPO Li (Lithium phenyl-2,4,6-trimethylbenzoylphosphinate) best initiate the photopolymerization process of the PEGDA monomer despite the proportion of water in the compositions. The initiating systems with GQDs blue also showed high potential, analogous to previous measurements. The lowest conversion was obtained for the PEGDA monomer with an initiating system consisting of a diaryliodonium salt in the form of SpeedCure 938, a co-initiator in the form of MDEA amine and water. In addition, the same compositions were used to print 3D materials using a Lumen X printer. The resulting prints were subjected to cytotoxicity tests and the results are described in paragraph Biological study of GQDs blue and GQDs properties using in vitro cell culture model.

The next stage of the research was to determine the optimum printing parameters (E0 and Cd) for the composition chosen for the 3D printing tests. Determining the layer curing time is very important, as too short a time can result in the resin not being cured sufficiently, while too long time can result in the resin being cured not only within the desired object but also around it. The quality and resolution of the print is dependent on the printing parameters, so it is crucial to determine the exposure time of the composition and the radiation power before printing. Determining the critical energy defines the minimum energy required to crosslink the light-curing resin. To determine the appropriate printing parameters, it is necessary to apply the Jacob's test. The Jacob's test was carried out for the composition GQDs blue (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA. To determine the printing parameters for the resin, slices with design dimensions of 1 × 1 × 0.3 mm were printed, with sample exposure times ranging from 25 s to 100 s. The print thickness (Cd) was then measured using a screw micrometer and the thickness measurements were repeated 5 times for each sample. Fig. 12
shows the results obtained.
From the above graph Cd = f(E0), an equation was derived from which the critical energy (Ec = 12.67 mJ/cm2) values for the photoresin containing GQDs blue were determined.

Currently, 3D printing is a topic that unites many researchers as it allows the creation of 3D materials in a controlled manner from multiple materials and with a predefined structure. We decided to test GQD as a part of the initiating system for the production of hydrogel materials by 3D printing with a bioprinter. Our previous work showed that GQDs prepared from citric acid alone are effective for 3D printing in the presence of suitable monomers and initiators. Thus far, the present work has shown that GQDs have fairly good potential to successfully initiate the photopolymerization process in 3D-VAT printing. Two hydrogel compositions A) GQDs blue (75 %), IOD(2 %), PEGDA and B) GQDs(50 %), blue, H2O(25 %), IOD(2 %), and monomer PEGDA were used for printing. A pyramid, designed using Fusion 360 software, was used as the printing object, and a Lumen X printer, which was optimized to build a solid hydrogel structure, was used (Fig. 13 ).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is a popular method for imaging the microstructures and morphologies of materials. In SEM, a low-energy electron beam is emitted onto a material and scans the surface of the sample. Several interactions occur as the beam reaches the material, leading to the emission of photons and electrons from or near the sample surface (Sampath Kumar, 2013). The prints were subjected to SEM analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 14
. Surface analysis showed that the print with the highest content of blue GQDs had the best quality. The resolution of the print is characterized by the best quality, and the pixels that were formed during the printing process are visible. Also interesting is the deeper surface texture, which appeared to be slightly wrinkled. This phenomenon may be due to the presence of water in this hydrogel and the fact that it evaporates, resulting in slight shrinkage of the print. It is worth noting the ripple tendency of the surface of the sample. Supplementary information includes an example of the SEM analysis of a printout without the presence of water in the composition. In this case, the surface of the sample was much less corrugated (Figure S20). The wavier and more heterogeneous surface was clearly visible in the subsequent analyses (green and red lines). In the SEM analysis of the reference print (water (75 %), IOD(2 %) and monomer PEGDA), it can be seen that the pixels are distorted. This may be because of the significant amount of water in the sample. In addition, the surfaces of the samples were examined for elements that may have been present in commercial blue GQDs. However, the analysis showed that only oxygen and carbon were present in the samples, which were sequentially derived from water and the polymer. Therefore, it can be deduced that the amount of GQDs in the composition from which the prints were made was so small that it could not be demonstrated by SEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological study of GQDs blue and GQDs properties using in vitro cell culture model
The effect of 3D printouts and selected hydrogel formulation components on non-cancerous cells’ proliferation
ce:section.ce:para: First, the printouts obtained as described in the previous section were examined for potential antiproliferative activity in non-cancerous CHO-K1 cells using the MTT assay. The samples were selected for biological studies owing to their compositions as follows (the polymerization kinetics of the resins are described in the Free-radical photopolymerization of acrylate monomers). The polymerization kinetics of the resins are described in Fig. 11.

The obtained data demonstrated that after 24 h of exposure, 1 and 2 prints caused the greatest cytotoxicity and showed similar effect of 60 % inhibition of cell growth (Fig. 15 ). At the same time, printout 3 also showed an antiproliferative effect in cells, with mean 40 % inhibition. In contrast, cells exposed to printouts 4 and 5 and the commercially available CELLINK formulation remained unaltered with respect to the control cells after 24 h for all endpoint measurements.

The constituents of printouts 1 and 2 contain the same photoinitiator, SpeedCure 938. Therefore, it is important to evaluate whether the antiproliferative activity of printouts depends on this component. As suggested by the safety data sheets, SpeedCure 938 may, in particular, inhibit living cells. Therefore, in the next step, the antiproliferative activity of a range of concentrations of the compound was measured in CHO-K1 cells. As presented in Fig. 16 , SpeedCure 938 demonstrated inhibitory potency and, in particular, decreased cell proliferation at concentrations of 0.1 mM/L and 1 mM/L. Moreover, the data obtained for TPO- and TPO-Li-based printouts did not show any toxic effects. These facts, together with the good water solubility of both photoinitiators TPO and TPO-Li, suggest that they can be used to produce non-toxic hydrogels that are potentially more biocompatible than SpeedCure 938-based formulations. Photoinitiators have been widely applied in the industry, and with their emerging role in biomedical applications, it is important to develop novel formulations with improved cytocompatibility.[54].

As shown in Fig. 17
B, GQDs blue and GQDs themselves had no adverse effect on CHO-K1 cell viability after 24 h of incubation. Only a slight decrease in cell proliferation was observed in the cells treated with the highest concentration of GQDs (100 ng/mL). However, microscopic observations of cell culture monolayers exposed to the samples did not confirm any disruption of cells (Fig. 17 A) which supports the assumption that the toxic effect of 1 and 2 printouts is attributed to SpeedCure 938 rather than graphene quantum dots.
To verify whether graphene quantum dots themselves do not cause any detrimental effects to other cells, a panel of normal and cancer cell lines was employed in the next part of the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of graphene quantum dots on proliferation of cells of various tissue origin
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison of the inhibitory activity of GQDs blue/GQDs and the well-known toxic drug doxorubicin demonstrated differences in the cytotoxic profile of the dots and the anticancer agent. Significant changes in the viability of normal and cancerous cell lines treated with both graphene quantum dots were measured as compared to control cells (Fig. 18 ). At the same time, 100 μM doxorubicin caused a substantial decrease in cell viability, which was observed in all tested cell lines (* p < 0.01 vs control). The obtained results should be considered with awareness of the potentially diverse mechanisms affecting this biological response. However, the obtained data are consistent with previous reports that graphene quantum dots have low toxicity and may potentially be widely used in the biomedical field.[55].

It should be underlined that for all the studied cell lines, graphene carbon dots had a minor effect on cell proliferation. The data obtained in the present study support the idea that commercially available blue GQDs and GQDs may be used for non-toxic and potentially biocompatible 3D objects and, possibly, to advance other applications such as biosensors that do not harm living cells. These findings can prompt the development of GQDs and their use in novel biomedical solutions. To advance the study and elucidate the effect of commercially available graphene quantum dots on living cells, we performed another experiment to reveal the mechanism involved in dot-cell interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study of cell survival upon graphene quantum dots treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the possible interactions of graphene quantum dots containing hydrogels with cells, it is essential to determine whether nanomaterials are characterized by their ability to cause programmed cell death. In this context, another experiment was performed to determine whether graphene quantum dots can induce apoptotic and necrotic cell death. Apoptosis is a crucial biological process that may occur in cells as a result of environmental signaling, particularly the interactions of cells with the substratum. In the pathological state, such activity may begin the death cascade, which leads to the progressive elimination of cells from the biomaterial.

As shown in Fig. 19 , neither of the tested graphene carbon dots did not elucidated apoptosis or necrosis in cells upon 24 h of exposure. Again, Doxorubicin at a concentration of 1 mM/L eliminated almost all living cells from the cell culture owing to necrosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cellular uptake of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence of GQDs is one of the most valuable properties for their potential use in living systems. In particular, the blue GQDs exhibited blue-light emission. Next, flow cytometry was employed to detect the ability of GQDs blue to emit fluorescence at the single-cell level, and appropriate controls were prepared to subtract the autofluorescence of cells.

In the present study, we demonstrate that graphene quantum dots efficiently initiate photopolymerization. However, a growing body of evidence suggests that the tested particles may also have potential applications as biosensors.[56] Therefore, in the experiments, we measured the fluorescence intensity of graphene carbon dots in a population of cells exposed to particles for 24 h. Hoechst and Syto-41, which are widely used in cell cycle and apoptosis studies as nuclear stains, were used as the reference dyes. As mentioned earlier, the tested graphene carbon dots had various sizes within the range of 50–1000 nm, which apparently limited their ability to cross cell membranes, especially larger GQDs. However, the data obtained showed that cells exposed to GQDs emitted blue fluorescence even at the lowest concentration of 10 ng/mL (Fig. 20 ). It should be noted that flow cytometry is a very sensitive technique that allows the detection of fluorescence at the level of a particular cell exposed to the tested samples. This result opens the way to further tests on the use of graphene quantum dots as components of hydrogels not for one, but for several applications.

Commercially available graphene quantum dots were used in this study. Their spectroscopic properties were determined, and initiating systems were developed for the photopolymerization of various acrylate monomers, leading to polymeric hydrogel materials. The same compositions were used in the 3D printing process to produce three-dimensional materials. The effectiveness of the developed GQD-based system was validated using digital light processing (DLP) technology. FTIR and 3D printing experiments did not conclusively prove what accounts for the potential of GQDs. It could be the graphene quantum dots as well as the fluorophore, the presence of which was found in the materials tested. As is well known, fluorophores have good spectroscopic properties and are able to efficiently initiate photopolymerization processes. However, as research has shown, carbon dots also possess this property, so the final effect of the GQDs is undoubtedly influenced by the action of the two species mentioned. Blue GQDs and GQDs were subjected to cytotoxic activity tests using a panel of normal and cancer cells. An in vitro study showed that commercially available graphene quantum dots do not inhibit living cells and do not express the ability to eliminate cells owing to apoptosis and necrosis, which would otherwise limit their use as biocompatible components. Moreover, despite the significant size of the dots, they can be efficiently transported into cells and retain their ability to emit blue light, as demonstrated by the measurement of fluorescence intensity at the level of a single cell. This opens up the possibility of a more advanced analysis of the use of graphene quantum dots as modern biosensors. We believe that this study makes an important contribution to the scientific literature. Moreover, they open up a new perspective for the application of GQDs in biological and cytotoxic research. In addition, the materials studied have the potential to become sensors in modern medical diagnostics. Biosensors continue to be a huge field of research, so there will certainly be an increasing number of proposals for their applications. In the future, graphene carbon dots may become the object of more intentional research in this direction.

Dominika Krok-Janiszewska: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Weronika Wielgus: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Patrycja Środa: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Małgorzata Tyszka-Czochara: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Petr Lepcio: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Resources. Wiktor Kasprzyk: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Joanna Ortyl: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Joanna Ortyl reports financial support was provided by National Science Centre. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was funded by National Science Centre (NCN, Poland) from budget funds for science in the years 2022–2026, as a research project under the “OPUS” program (“Emerging strategy approaches for the design and functionalization of carbon dots as multifunctional, dynamic, green systems photoinitiators and photocatalysts involved in photopolymerisation processes”; project no: UMO-2021/41/B/ST5/04533).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2024.113052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J Photochem. Photobiol. C: Photochem. Rev.
